title: Midshaft humerus fracture: deep brachial artery
vignette: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department due to right arm pain after a fall. While rock climbing at a nearby national park, the patient lost his grip and fell approximately 3 m (10 ft) onto the boulders below. He says, "I landed on my right arm and heard something snap." The patient did not strike his head or lose consciousness. He has no chronic medical conditions and takes no daily medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows extensive bruising over the lateral right arm and a total inability to extend the right wrist. X-ray reveals a midshaft fracture of the right humerus. Which of the following arteries is most likely to be injured in this patient?

clues.text: extensive bruising over the lateral right arm
clues.note: Soft-tissue trauma over the arm localizes the injury to the mid-upper arm, consistent with a humeral shaft injury and damage to the structures coursing there.

clues.text: total inability to extend the right wrist
clues.note: Inability to extend the wrist (wrist drop) is the hallmark of radial nerve injury - the radial nerve supplies the wrist and finger extensors. The radial nerve runs in the radial (spiral) groove of the mid-humerus, so wrist drop localizes the lesion there.

clues.text: midshaft fracture of the right humerus
clues.note: A midshaft humeral fracture is the classic injury to the radial groove, where the radial nerve and its companion DEEP BRACHIAL (profunda brachii) artery travel together along the posterior humerus - so both are at risk.

whatMatters: Boil it down: a midshaft humeral fracture with WRIST DROP (radial nerve injury). The radial nerve and the DEEP BRACHIAL (profunda brachii) artery travel together in the radial (spiral) groove along the posterior humerus, so a midshaft fracture injures both. Answer C.
strategy: For 'which artery/nerve is injured,' localize by the FRACTURE SITE and pair the nerve with its companion artery. Midshaft humerus -> radial nerve + deep brachial artery (spiral groove). Surgical neck -> axillary nerve + posterior circumflex humeral artery. Supracondylar -> median nerve + brachial artery. The wrist-drop sign confirms the radial nerve, i.e., the midshaft.

eliminations.label: brachial artery - anteromedial, injured at the elbow
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the brachial artery is the main artery of the arm, running anteromedially; it is classically vulnerable at the elbow (eg, SUPRACONDYLAR fractures). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it lies anteromedially, not in the posterior spiral groove with the radial nerve, so a midshaft fracture with wrist drop points to the deep brachial instead. ✅ Correct if: a supracondylar humeral fracture compromising distal brachial flow.

eliminations.label: common interosseous artery - a forearm vessel
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the common interosseous artery is a short branch of the ulnar artery in the proximal FOREARM. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is a forearm vessel, far from a midshaft humeral fracture. ✅ Correct if: a proximal forearm injury.

eliminations.label: posterior circumflex humeral - surgical neck, not midshaft
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the posterior circumflex humeral artery branches from the axillary artery and encircles the SURGICAL NECK with the axillary nerve (quadrangular space). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is injured with surgical-neck fractures or shoulder dislocations (-> axillary nerve, lost shoulder abduction), not midshaft fractures with wrist drop. ✅ Correct if: a surgical-neck humerus fracture or anterior shoulder dislocation.

eliminations.label: radial collateral artery - distal branch, less likely (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the radial collateral artery is a terminal branch of the deep brachial artery that continues with the radial nerve near the lateral elbow. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it arises at the LOWER end of the spiral groove, so a MIDSHAFT fracture is more likely to injure the parent deep brachial artery itself than this distal branch. The trap is choosing the smaller distal branch over the main vessel at the fracture site. ✅ Correct if: a more distal injury along the radial nerve near the elbow.

reasoning: Midshaft humerus fracture + wrist drop (radial nerve injury)
The radial nerve runs with the deep brachial (profunda brachii) artery in the spiral groove
A midshaft fracture injures both structures
Artery most likely injured -> deep brachial artery -> answer C
mechanism: The radial nerve and its companion DEEP BRACHIAL (profunda brachii) artery wind together in the radial (spiral) groove along the posterior midshaft of the humerus. A midshaft humeral fracture therefore classically injures both, producing WRIST DROP (loss of the wrist/finger extensors and brachioradialis) and risking the deep brachial artery. Other humeral levels pair differently: surgical neck -> axillary nerve + posterior circumflex humeral artery; supracondylar -> median nerve + brachial artery. The radial collateral artery is only a distal branch of the deep brachial near the elbow.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Anatomy/MSK > humeral fractures and nerve-artery pairings: midshaft -> radial nerve + deep brachial artery (spiral groove); surgical neck -> axillary nerve + posterior circumflex; supracondylar -> median nerve + brachial artery.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: (1) localize the lesion (wrist drop = radial nerve = spiral groove = midshaft), (2) name the artery that travels with that nerve there (deep brachial). Pair the nerve to its companion vessel.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on wrist drop, radial/spiral groove, deep brachial (profunda brachii) artery, surgical neck, supracondylar, posterior circumflex humeral? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Radial collateral artery (E) is the near-miss - it travels with the radial nerve too, but as a DISTAL branch near the elbow. A midshaft fracture hits the parent deep brachial artery. Pick the vessel at the fracture level.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: An anatomy diagram shows the deep brachial artery and radial nerve along the humerus. Build the level map: surgical neck (axillary n. + posterior circumflex), midshaft/spiral groove (radial n. + deep brachial), supracondylar (median n. + brachial). Match the fracture site to the pair.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.back: Radial nerve + deep brachial (profunda brachii) artery

keywords.front: Companion artery of the radial nerve in the arm

keywords.back: Axillary nerve + posterior circumflex humeral artery
Distal branch of the deep brachial near the elbow

firstAid.detail: Anatomy/MSK > midshaft humerus -> radial nerve + deep brachial artery (spiral groove); wrist drop.
firstAid.location: Anatomy/MSK > midshaft humerus -> radial nerve + deep brachial artery (spiral groove); wrist drop.

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > brachial artery (supracondylar/elbow), posterior circumflex humeral (surgical neck), forearm arteries (common interosseous).
firstAid.location: Anatomy > brachial artery (supracondylar/elbow), posterior circumflex humeral (surgical neck), forearm arteries (common interosseous).

firstAid.detail: Neuro/MSK > radial nerve injury patterns (axilla vs spiral groove vs forearm) and corresponding deficits.
firstAid.location: Neuro/MSK > radial nerve injury patterns (axilla vs spiral groove vs forearm) and corresponding deficits.

memoryHook: The radial nerve and deep brachial artery spiral together - snap the midshaft, you hit both (wrist drop + deep brachial).
